hook: You already know that eigenvalues and eigenvectors reveal the 'skeleton' of a linear operator — but what happens when a matrix stubbornly refuses to be diagonalizable? This unit uncovers the deeper structure hiding beneath those cases, showing that every operator over $\mathbb{C}$ can be understood through a beautifully organized canonical form. How close to diagonal can we always get, and what symmetry can we impose when we work with inner products?
concept: The unifying idea is that every linear operator on a complex vector space has a canonical 'simplest possible form,' and that understanding these forms — from the block structure of the Jordan form to the symmetry of unitary operators — gives you complete control over the geometry and behavior of linear maps. Formally, this unit develops the spectral theory of operators via the decomposition of vector spaces into invariant subspaces.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Generalized Eigenvectors and the Jordan Form (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to construct generalized eigenvectors when ordinary eigenvectors fall short, building the foundation for decomposing any complex operator into Jordan blocks.
Master the structure of nilpotent operators — those for which $N^k = 0$ for some $k$ — and see how their simple cycle structure is the key building block inside every Jordan block.
Apply a rigorous proof of the Jordan Normal Form theorem, gaining the ability to determine the exact Jordan structure of any operator from its eigenvalue data.
You will learn to express any linear operator in Rational Canonical Form using invariant factors, obtaining a canonical description that works over any field — not just $\mathbb{C}$.
You will characterize unitary operators as the transformations that perfectly preserve inner products and lengths, and learn to recognize and work with unitary matrices via the condition $U^*U = I$.
